trajectory_summary: William was pre-sold before the call started — he had already done DIY solar on his camper, knew panel counts, and wanted whole-house coverage. The rep's job was largely to collect data and book. The bill was captured (via photo) and an appointment was set for the next day at 12:30. However, the call's structural execution was thin: the intro skipped the legitimacy beat, verify was abandoned after one exchange, and the button-up delivered only one of six required elements. The appointment exists but is fragile — William missed the first delivery of the appointment pitch due to his hearing aid, the closer has no context on the unrepresentative bill data, and no phone/computer/no-driving anchors were set.
outcome_dispute: The call produced a booked appointment, but the button-up was incomplete enough that the closer will walk in without standard anchors (phone format, computer requirement, 'is that fair' handshake). Whether Ion's appointments are phone-only or in-person is not definitively established by the codex — the rep's 'pop by' framing may reflect an in-person format for this market. Manager should verify appointment format before treating this as a protocol violation. The booking itself is real; the fragility is structural.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Button-up completeness — five of six required elements skipped
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Tomorrow would be fine. Yeah. I'll I'll I'll be here.
primary_coaching_focus.why: William confirmed the appointment, which means the rep had a willing customer and a clear opening to run the full button-up. Of the six required elements, only appointment_datetime_confirmed was delivered. The one-hour block, format specification, no-driving anchor, computer requirement, 'is that fair' handshake, and text follow-up promise were all absent. On a warm, cooperative customer like William, these elements take under 60 seconds and directly predict show rate. The habit of stopping at time confirmation — rather than running the full close — is the single most actionable gap on this call.

key_moments.quote: Hey. I'm doing pretty good. Yeah, William. I was just reaching out from Ion Solar in regards to a solar design for the home. Did you put in that request?
key_moments.stewart_read: The intro omits the legitimacy beat entirely. No Tesla framing, no state mention, no 'certified installer' anchor. The rep goes straight to 'solar design for the home' without establishing why Ion is calling or why William should trust the call. For a warm, pre-sold customer like William this didn't cost the call — but the beat is hard-required per the codex and its absence is a habit that will matter on colder leads.

key_moments.quote: Oh, okay. Yeah. We can definitely do that then.
key_moments.stewart_read: The moment William clarifies he wants whole-house solar, the rep confirms and pivots directly to bill collection — skipping all six remaining verify items (title/co-owners, residence length, interest in solar, utility company, roof type/age, prior designs). The reactive read is that the rep saw a willing customer and rushed to data collection. The strategic read — that William was pre-sold and the rep intentionally compressed verify — is plausible but unsupported: no verify items are collected at any point before or after this pivot, and lead type is unknown (paid vs. organic), which the codex flags as changing the coaching read on bill-collection timing. On a paid lead, bill collection is required before booking; on an organic lead, verify completion matters more. Without lead-type metadata, the coaching severity is uncertain — but the verify abandonment is observable regardless.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. Hold on. Kilowatt usage for the the years?
key_moments.stewart_read: William's willingness to immediately look up his bill — 'Hold on' — is an enthusiasm signal. He is not resistant, not deflecting, not asking why the rep needs it. He is actively trying to help. The rep captures this correctly by asking for the monthly readout, but never anchors the bill emotionally. The bill comes in at $90.80 / 80 kWh — atypically low because William hasn't been living in the home. The rep captures the number and moves on without the flip ('what you're paying even when you're barely there is the floor — imagine when you're back full time'). The low bill is actually a leverage point for a future-state anchor, and it was walked past entirely.

key_moments.quote: I'm trying to go by by the side. 80 one, two, three.
key_moments.stewart_read: William is attempting to estimate kWh by eyeballing bar heights on a graph — a low-tech workaround that signals he is not comfortable with precise digital readback. The rep correctly redirects ('No. No. You're all good. I don't want you to have to worry about trying to read it off') and the photo path ultimately works (bill received at 04:43). The pivot happens within the call's natural flow, not after 8-15 minutes of friction. This is adequate handling of the tech-friction scenario — the codex's 2-minute pivot threshold is met. The residual coaching gap is that the bill data captured is acknowledged as unrepresentative and this is never flagged for the closer.
